coredata.dc:title: Boron-doped SiOC anode material with an yolk shell multistage structure for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lei
Ye, Qiaoqiao
Han, Chengxuan
Pu, Minjie
Ma, Hongying
Weng, Xiuji
Yang, Jianping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a boron-doped yolk-shell structured B-YS@C was constructed on the surface of SiOC anodes with yolk-shell structures via a combination of redox processes, freeze-drying, and heteroatom infiltration. Multilevel pore formation was achieved by controlling experimental conditions. The structure and morphology of the materials were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results demonstrate that boron was successfully doped into the material structure, and the yolk-shell structure exhibits good integrity and multilevel pore characteristics. Electrochemical tests show that the boron-doped yolk-shell multilevel structured anode composite delivers excellent specific capacity (473 mAh g−1), rate performance, and cycling stability (retaining 99.9 % average coulombic efficiency after 300 cycles) at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. Compared with YS@C, the B-YS@C electrode maintains a specific capacity of 473 mAh g−1 and 99.9 % average coulombic efficiency with almost no capacity decay after 300 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, demonstrating ultra-strong cycling stability. These results highlight its broad application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron doping technology
SiOC-based anodes
Yolk-shell hierarchical structure
High-energy-density batteries
Redox process

Synthesis of boron-doped yolk shell multistage structure (B-YS@C)
Characterization methods of materials and electrochemical tests

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM and (c, d) TEM images of B-YS@C.
(a) Thermogravimetric curve, (b) infrared spectrum, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of B-YS@C.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution of B-YS@C.
(a) XPS spectrum, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Si 2p, (c) c 1 s, and (d) b 1 s for B-YS@C.
CV curves in the first three cycles of B-YS@C.
Initial charge-discharge curves of the B-YS@C electrode during the first five cycles at 0.1 A g−1.
(a) Rate performance and (b, d) cycling performance of the B-YS@C electrode, and (c) comparison of cycling performance between B-YS@C and YS@C.
(a) GITT curves of B-YS@C, and (b) Li+ diffusion coefficients of B-YS@C and YS@C calculated by GITT.

(a) CV curves of the B-YS@C electrode at different scanning rates from 0.1 to 2.0 mV s−1, (b) the corresponding linear relationships between peak current and scanning rate, and (c) the percentage of capacitive contribution at different scanning rates.
Impedance performance of B-YS@C for the different cycle.
SEM images of the B-YS@C electrode (a) before cycling and (b) after 300 cycles.
Comparison of the performances of B-YS@C with the reported SiOx-based anode materials.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped SiOC anode material with an yolk shell multistage structure for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Zhuogu Technology Co., Ltd., Zhengzhou 450007, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chen Lei (1984-), male, holds a doctorate and is an associate professor. His main research directions are energy chemical engineering and new energy materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a boron-doped yolk-shell structured B-YS@C was constructed on the surface of SiOC anodes with yolk-shell structures via a combination of redox processes, freeze-drying, and heteroatom infiltration. Multilevel pore formation was achieved by controlling experimental conditions. The structure and morphology of the materials were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results demonstrate that boron was successfully doped into the material structure, and the yolk-shell structure exhibits good integrity and multilevel pore characteristics. Electrochemical tests show that the boron-doped yolk-shell multilevel structured anode composite delivers excellent specific capacity (473 mAh g−1), rate performance, and cycling stability (retaining 99.9 % average coulombic efficiency after 300 cycles) at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. Compared with YS@C, the B-YS@C electrode maintains a specific capacity of 473 mAh g−1 and 99.9 % average coulombic efficiency with almost no capacity decay after 300 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, demonstrating ultra-strong cycling stability. These results highlight its broad application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries have proven to be strong candidates for portable and grid energy storage systems due to their high energy density, scalability, and stable cycling characteristics [1,2]. The research on SiOC anode materials began in the 1990s, when Professor Jeff Dahn's research group at Dalhousie University in Canada first used SiOC as a anode material for lithium-ion batteries [3]. SiOC material has the characteristics of high specific capacity and excellent cycling performance, and has been widely studied by researchers. However, in practical applications, its capacity decay is too fast, capacity retention is poor, and the first coulombic efficiency is low [4–7].

Researchers have made significant efforts to improve the charge-discharge rates of silicon-based anodes, mainly focusing on geometric modification, such as reducing the size of silicon particles to the nanoscale to shorten the transport pathways of electrons and Li+, and compositional modification, such as fabricating various silicon-carbon and silicon/conductive polymer composites to enhance surface electron conductivity [8,9]. Although exciting results have been achieved through these strategies, the inherent semiconducting nature of silicon leads to its low electrical conductivity, which limits further improvements in rate capability [10,11]. Heteroatom doping can endow silicon-based anodes with various new electronic and physicochemical properties. The introduction of heteroatoms (nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, sulfur) into composites can alter the electronic structure and chemical bonds at the composite interfaces, significantly enhancing the electrochemical performance of silicon-based anode materials [12,13]. As a graphitization catalyst, boron (1 s²2 s²2p¹) can significantly enhance the graphitization of carbon. Boron doping can modify the electronic properties of the graphite framework, facilitating the transport behavior of lithium ions [14,15]. Incorporating boron into silicon usually generates stress in the surrounding silicon atom network, significantly reducing the interfacial resistance. High-temperature treatment increases the crystallinity of silicon, further resisting surface oxidation. Previous research on high-performance silicon-based anodes primarily focused on constructing complex stacked structures, but the introduction of heteroatoms is crucial for fabricating high-performance silicon-based structures with diverse morphologies and functions [16–19].

In this paper, we propose a strategy for doping heteroatom boron on the surface of the yolk-shell structure. To optimize the yolk-shell structured materials, redox processes, freeze-drying, and heteroatom infiltration are employed to introduce boron atoms onto the surface of the yolk-shell structure before carbon coating. This hierarchical structure design integrates the advantages of each structural component: (1) The cavities/porous structure of the yolk-shell provides space for the volume expansion of silicon; (2) The introduction of boron atoms improves the conductivity of the yolk-shell structure, thereby enhancing the coulombic efficiency; (3) The carbon coating enhances conductivity and provides a supporting framework.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of yolk shell structure YS@C
ce:section.ce:para: YS@C were prepared by a modified sol-gel method according to the previous literature [8]. In a 200 mL three-neck flask, 70 mL of deionized water, 30 mL of absolute ethanol, and 200 mg of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were added and stirred at 35 °C for 10 min. Then 1 mL of ammonia water (25 wt%) was introduced. After reacting for 1 h, 0.25 mL of 1,4-bis(triethoxysilyl)benzene (BTEB) was added dropwise, and the reaction proceeded for 12 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged and dried. Subsequently, 30 mL of absolute ethanol, 70 mL of deionized water, and 200 mg of CTAB were added again. After stirring for 10 min, 1 mL of ammonia water (25 wt%) was added, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h before introducing 0.25 mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS). Following a 12-hour reaction, the product was centrifuged and dried to obtain core-shell structures. The as-prepared core-shell structures were then immersed in 30 mL of Na2CO3 solution (2.4 M), stirred for 3 h, and centrifuged/dried to yield yolk-shell structured YS-PMOs.

Next, 1.5 g of YS-PMOs was added to a mixture of 280 mL deionized water, 120 mL absolute ethanol, and 800 mg CTAB. After reacting for 10 min, 4 mL of ammonia water (25 wt%) was added. After 0.5 h, 700 mg of resorcinol was introduced, followed by 0.5 h of reaction and subsequent addition of 1 mL of formaldehyde. The reaction was maintained for 24 h, after which the product was centrifuged and dried. Finally, the sample was calcined at 800 °C for 3 h under a 10 % H2/Ar mixed atmosphere to obtain YS@C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron-doped yolk shell multistage structure (B-YS@C)
ce:section.ce:para: For boron doping, 20 mg of YS-PMOs powder was mixed with 6 mg of boric acid in 20 mL of deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 1 h and then freeze-dried using liquid nitrogen to obtain a white powder. Next, 1.5 g of this powder was dispersed in a solution of 70 mL deionized water, 30 mL absolute ethanol, and 200 mg CTAB. After 10 min of stirring, 1 mL of ammonia solution (25 wt%) was added. After 30 min, 200 mg of resorcinol and 0.25 mL of formaldehyde were sequentially introduced. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 24 h, after which the product was centrifuged and dried. Finally, the powder was calcined at 800 °C for 3 h under a 10 % H2/Ar atmosphere to obtain the boron-doped yolk-shell structured composite, denoted as B-YS@C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization methods of materials and electrochemical tests
ce:section.ce:para: The surface microstructure of the samples was analyzed and characterized using a MAIA3LM field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). The morphology of the samples was characterized by a Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (TEM) from Thermo Fisher Scientific, and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed using accessories attached to this TEM. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE instrument with a CuKα radiation source (λ=0.15418 nm) over a scanning range of 10°-80°. The surface properties of the samples were measured using an autosorb iQ nitrogen adsorption-desorption instrument, and the specific surface area was analyzed based on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) multi-molecular layer adsorption formula.The as-prepared active material, conductive carbon black (conductive agent), and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (binder) were mixed and ground in an agate mortar for 30 min at a mass ratio of 7: 1.5: 1.5. Subsequently, a small amount of deionized water was added, and the mixture was further ground in a planetary shaker, with the amount of deionized water added adjusted according to the viscosity of the mixture. After thorough mixing, the mixture was coated onto the surface of a copper foil, vacuum-dried at 80 °C for 12 h, and then the copper foil was cut into circular discs with a diameter of 10 mm. These discs were pressed into tablets using a powder tableting machine at a pressure of 15–20 MPa to serve as working electrodes. The separator used was Celgard 2300, and the electrolyte was 1 mol/L LiPF6/(EC + DMC + EMC) (volume ratio 1: 1: 1). The batteries were then sealed with a sealer and left to stand for 7–8 h. Battery performance tests were carried out using a Shenzhen Neware battery testing system, including constant current charge-discharge tests within a voltage range of 0.01–1.5 V. Cyclic voltammetry and alternating current impedance tests were performed using a CHI 660 electrochemical workstation from Shanghai Chenhua.

The electron microscopy images of the boron-doped yolk-shell hierarchical structure are shown in Fig. 1. Uniformly sized spheres are interconnected, with an average diameter of approximately 300 nm. A thin and uniform carbon layer coats the surface. The specific yolk-shell structure can be observed via TEM: the interior near the outer shell is gradually etched, and the expanded voids form the "albumen" part of the yolk-shell structure.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the thermogravimetry was used to get the carbon content of B-YS@C. The carbon content of B-YS@C is 52.4 %. The lost carbon comes from two sources: the phenolic resin in the coating layer and the internal siloxane. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis (Fig. 2b) reveals a peak assigned to CC at 1500 cm−1. B-YS@C exhibits an amorphous state. In Fig. 2c, the XRD pattern of B-YS@C exhibits amorphous diffraction peaks, with a broad peak in the range of 20°-30° representing the 002 peak of carbon, and a weaker broad peak at 40°-45° corresponding to the 100 peak of carbon. The two Raman peaks in Fig. 2d show at 1340 and 1600 cm−1 for the amorphous carbon of B-YS@C. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands is used to assess the graphitization degree of the carbon material. The calculation indicates that the graphitization degree (ID/IG) of B-YS@C is 1.06.

As shown in Fig. 3, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption test (BET) characterized the mesoporous structure and surface condition of B-YS@C. The adsorption-desorption isotherm of B-YS@C exhibits a typical Type IV shape with an H4 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of irregular slit-like mesopores and macropores. The pore size distribution calculated by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method shows that the voids in the electrode material are centered at 2.2 nm.

The elemental composition and chemical states of B-YS@C were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum in Fig. 4a shows that B-YS@C is predominantly composed of carbon, oxygen, boron, and silicon. The fitting of the Si 2p peak in B-YS@C is displayed in Fig. 4b. According to literature reports, the three deconvoluted peaks at 103.4, 102.3, and 101.4 eV are assigned to Si4+, Si3+, and Si2+, respectively.[8] The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks at approximately 283 and 284 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C and C-O bond carbons, respectively. The B 1 s spectrum exhibits four deconvoluted peaks at 196, 193, 190, and 185 eV, attributed to B from BO3, BCO3, BC2O, and BC3, respectively. These provide conclusive evidence for the successful doping of boron, with the calculated boron atomic doping content in B-YS@C being 4.95 at%. As reported in previous literature, it is generally recognized that an optimal boron doping content (approximately 5 at%) can achieve the best electrochemical performance [18].

The electrochemical performance of B-YS@C was investigated after assembling it into a half-cell. First, the lithiation process of B-YS@C was examined. Fig. 5 shows the first three cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of B-YS@C within the voltage range of 0.01–2.0 V at a scanning rate of 0.1 mV s−1. The reduction peak at approximately 1.4 V during the first lithiation of B-YS@C is attributed to the irreversible reaction between SiOx and Li, forming Li2O, lithium silicate, and crystalline silicon. The generated crystalline silicon then undergoes an alloying reaction with Li at around 0.1 V to form LixSi alloy. The peak observed at approximately 0.55 V corresponds to the decomposition of the electrolyte and the formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film. After the first cycle, the crystalline silicon transforms into amorphous silicon. Therefore, in the second cycle, the newly observed reduction peak at approximately 0.38 V corresponds to the lithiation process of amorphous silicon. Additionally, the CV curves of the 2nd-5th cycles overlap well, indicating good reversibility and stability of the B-YS@C material. The gradual increase in peak intensity during the initial cycles suggests that some crystalline silicon remains in the silicon particles, which is common for micro-sized silicon anodes.

As shown in Fig. 6, the initial specific charge and discharge capacities of B-YS@C are 865.10 and 1615.07 mAh g−1, respectively, with a corresponding initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 54 %. This indicates that the boron-doped yolk-shell hierarchical structure exhibits a favorable initial coulombic efficiency.

When assembled as an anode, the B-YS@C material also exhibits excellent rate capability ( Fig. 7a). The specific capacities of the B-YS@C electrode at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1 are 783, 645, 518, 435, and 352 mAh g−1, respectively. Notably, once the current density is restored to 0.1 A g−1, the capacity recovers to 788 mAh g−1, likely because the outer surface of B-YS@C is a carbon layer-during initial cycling, primarily the outer carbon participates in the reaction, resulting in lower capacity. After cycling for a period, the inner active materials engage in the reaction, causing a slight capacity increase. This capacity rise during a certain number of cycles is also evident in subsequent long-term cycling tests. After 100 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 0.2 A g−1, B-YS@C retains a capacity of 602 mAh g−1. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1, B-YS@C exhibits a specific capacity of 506 mAh g−1(Fig. 7b). Compared with the boron-undoped yolk-shell structure YS@C(Fig. 7c), YS@C shows specific capacities of 530 and 436 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles at current densities of 0.2 and 0.5 A g−1, respectively. These results indicate that the electrochemical performance of the boron-doped yolk-shell structure is significantly improved: boron doping further enhances lithium storage capacity, providing high reversible capacity and remarkable cycling stability. More importantly, B-YS@C demonstrates ultra-stable long-term cycling stability under fast charge-discharge conditions. B-YS@C was first activated for 5 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 and then cycled at a high current density of 1 A g−1. Even after 600 cycles, B-YS@C still maintains a high specific capacity of 414 mAh g−1. From the 6th cycle onward, the coulombic efficiency of B-YS@C remains above 99 %. Furthermore, the B-YS@C anode developed in this study exhibits markedly superior energy storage performance in comparison to the majority of SiOx-based anode materials reported in the existing literature ( Table 1).

To investigate the relationship between the diffusion process of substances and charge transfer in the B-YS@C electrode, a galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was employed ( Fig. 8). As shown in Fig. 8b, compared with the boron-undoped yolk-shell structure YS@C, the Li+ diffusion coefficient of the B-YS@C anode is improved, demonstrating the role of boron doping in enhancing ion/electron conductivity.

The lithium ion kinetics of B-YS@C were analyzed by measuring CV curves at different scanning rates ( Fig. 9). Notably, a higher contribution of the pseudocapacitive storage mechanism indicates faster lithium ion migration in the electrode material. The b-value obtained by fitting the oxidation peak in Fig. 9a is 0.95, suggesting that the lithium storage mechanism of the B-YS@C electrode is predominantly dominated by a pseudocapacitive control process. As shown in Fig. 9c, with the increase in scanning rate, the contribution of the pseudocapacitive-controlled lithium storage process gradually increases. At a scanning rate of 2.0 mV s−1, the pseudocapacitive contribution reaches 92.2 %. Under high scanning rates, the pseudocapacitive control mechanism enables rapid lithium ion insertion into B-YS@C.

The superior electrochemical performance of B-YS@C can also be interpreted through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) ( Fig. 10). With electrode activation caused by charge-discharge cycles, the semicircle radius of B-YS@C decreases as the number of cycles increases. Compared with the boron-undoped yolk-shell structure, the boron-doped B-YS@C electrode exhibits lower charge transfer resistance, thereby enhancing its rate capability.

The integrity of the B-YS@C electrode before and after cycling was characterized ( Fig. 11). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the morphological changes of the electrode before and after 300 cycles. The electrode exhibits a complete and smooth morphology before cycling. After 300 cycles, although structural degradation and small cracks appear, the electrode still maintains good structural integrity.

In summary, a boron-doped yolk-shell hierarchical structure was successfully prepared by introducing boron atoms via freeze-drying. The boron atoms significantly enhance the electrical conductivity of the yolk-shell structure, and the construction of the hierarchical structure enables the coexistence of high-rate capability and long-cycle performance. EIS measurements show that the charge transfer resistance of the B-YS@C electrode is lower after boron doping, thereby improving the rate capability. When used as an anode for lithium-ion batteries, the B-YS@C electrode maintains a specific capacity of 473 mAh g−1 after 300 cycles at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, compared with the boron-undoped YS@C, indicating that boron doping improves the conductivity and rate performance of the yolk-shell structure. Boron doping not only optimizes the electronic structure of the material and improves the electrical conductivity, but also the yolk-shell hierarchical structure provides fast channels for ion transport and effectively alleviates the problem of volume expansion. The design of this boron-doped yolk-shell hierarchical structure provides new ideas for the development of high-performance anode materials and is expected to be widely used in lithium-ion batteries and other fields.

Lei Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qiaoqiao Ye: Investigation, Data curation. Chengxuan Han: Writing – original draft. Minjie Pu: Investigation, Data curation. Hongying Ma: Investigation. Xiuji Weng: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors disclose any financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that could inappropriately influence (bias) their work or state. There are no interests to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Horizontal project of Henan University of Engineering (HKJ2024146).
